narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on key indicators from your academic record and test scores to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 32 is lower than the usual 36.70, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 3 are notably below the averages around 5.5-5.7, placing you in the lower third of your peers. The model estimates a 46.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for rejection since the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the lower third of your school's grades signaled weaker late-stage academic performance compared to typical successful bar takers. Next, your middle family income level actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because students from similar brackets often show resilience and success rates above those from lower incomes. Your LSAT score was the third biggest influence, also nudging things positively since, while below average, it still demonstrated some foundational aptitude aligned with passers. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by that Year 3 performance—outweighed other details like your full-time study plans, tipping the scales against admission. Compared to typical students who get approved, you lag most in those decile rankings, where stronger mid-to-upper performers dominate the pass group. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to 37+, strengthening your upper-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your growth. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at this or other schools. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, you can rewrite this story.
